title: Nephrodium aquilinum
taxonomy: Nephrodium aquilinum is an extinct genus of ferns, and its classification is as follows: it belongs to the division Pteridophyta, class Polypodiopsida, and family Matoniaceae. The genus Nephrodium is considered to be part of the family Matoniaceae, and it is closely related to the genus Matonia. The classification of Nephrodium aquilinum is based on its fossilized remains and the characteristics of its fronds and spores. The plant is known to have lived during the Mesozoic Era, and its classification is an important part of understanding the evolution of ferns. Further research is needed to confirm the classification of Nephrodium aquilinum and its relationship to other fern genera.
uses: Nephrodium aquilinum, also known as the Lady Fern or Common Lady Fern, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's rhizome and roots are said to have been used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other ailments. In some cultures, it was used as a treatment for respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and coughs. The plant's medicinal properties are believed to be due to its rich content of saponins, flavonoids, and other bioactive compounds. However, further research is needed to fully understand its potential medicinal uses and benefits.

side_effects: Nephrodium aquilinum, also known as the Lady fern, is not typically used as a medicinal plant. intro: Nephrodium aquilinum, also known as the Lady Fern or Buckler Fern, is a perennial fern species native to Europe and North America. The Lady Fern has been used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, which include reducing inflammation, soothing respiratory issues, and acting as an anti-oxidant. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, coumarins, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Nephrodium aquilinum include teas, infusions, and tinctures, often used to treat coughs, colds, and other respiratory ailments.
